section.title: Parables of Prayer and the Rich Ruler
section.sectionRationale: Jesus teaches persistent prayer through a widow who wears down an unjust judge, and true prayer through a tax collector who simply begs for mercy and goes home justified while the proud Pharisee does not. He welcomes little children as the pattern of the kingdom, then watches a rich ruler turn away sorrowful, for it is easier for a camel to pass through a needle's eye than for the rich to enter, though what is impossible with man is possible with God. He foretells His passion a third time, and a blind beggar who will not be silenced receives his sight and follows.
section.thesis: The kingdom is entered not by the self-assured but by those who pray without losing heart, cry for mercy, receive it like a child, and let go of everything to follow Jesus, who is going up to Jerusalem to be killed and to rise, opening blind eyes along the way.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the persistent widow; the Pharisee and the tax collector; receiving the kingdom like a child; the rich ruler and the cost of following; and the third passion prediction with the healing of the blind beggar.

units.label: The persistent widow
units.note: pray at all times and do not lose heart

verses.text: Then Jesus told them a parable about their need to pray at all times and not {{enkakeo|lose heart}}:
“In a certain town there was a judge who neither feared God nor respected men.
And there was a widow in that town who kept appealing to him, ‘Give me justice against my adversary.’
For a while he refused, but later he said to himself, ‘Though I neither fear God nor respect men,
yet because this widow keeps pestering me, I will give her justice. Otherwise, she will wear me out with her perpetual requests.’”
And the Lord said, “Listen to the words of the unjust judge.
Will not God bring about justice for His elect who cry out to Him day and night? Will He delay in helping them?
I tell you, He will promptly carry out justice on their behalf. Nevertheless, when the Son of Man comes, will He find faith on earth?”

units.label: The Pharisee and the tax collector
units.note: God, have mercy on me, a sinner

verses.text: To some who trusted in their own righteousness and viewed others with contempt, He also told this parable:
“Two men went up to the temple to pray. One was a Pharisee and the other a tax collector.
The Pharisee stood by himself and prayed, ‘God, I thank You that I am not like other men, swindlers, evildoers, adulterers, or even like this tax collector.
I fast twice a week and pay tithes of all that I acquire.’
But the tax collector stood at a distance, unwilling even to lift up his eyes to heaven. Instead, he beat his breast and said, ‘God, {{hilaskomai|have mercy}} on me, a sinner!’
I tell you, this man, rather than the Pharisee, went home {{dikaioo|justified}}. For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but the one who humbles himself will be exalted.”

units.label: Receiving the kingdom like a child
units.note: the kingdom belongs to such as these

verses.text: Now people were even bringing their babies to Jesus for Him to place His hands on them. And when the disciples saw this, they rebuked those who brought them.
But Jesus called the children to Him and said, “Let the little children come to Me, and do not hinder them! For the kingdom of God belongs to such as these.
Truly I tell you, anyone who does not receive the kingdom of God like a little child will never enter it.”

units.label: The rich ruler and the cost of following
units.note: what is impossible with man is possible with God

verses.text: Then a certain ruler asked Him, “Good Teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life?”
“Why do you call Me good?” Jesus replied. “No one is good except God alone.
You know the commandments: ‘Do not commit adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not bear false witness, honor your father and mother.’”
“All these I have kept from my youth,” he said.
On hearing this, Jesus told him, “You still lack one thing: Sell everything you own and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow Me.”
But when the ruler heard this, he became very sad, because he was extremely wealthy.
Seeing the man’s sadness, Jesus said, “How hard it is for the rich to enter the kingdom of God!
Indeed, it is easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God.”
Those who heard this asked, “Who then can be saved?”
But Jesus said, “What is impossible with man is possible with God.”
“Look,” said Peter, “we have left all we had to follow You.”
“Truly I tell you,” Jesus replied, “no one who has left home or wife or brothers or parents or children for the sake of the kingdom of God
will fail to receive many times more in this age, and in the age to come, eternal life.”

units.label: The passion foretold and the blind beggar
units.note: Son of David, have mercy on me

verses.text: Then Jesus took the Twelve aside and said to them, “Look, we are going up to Jerusalem, and everything the prophets have written about the Son of Man will be fulfilled.
He will be delivered over to the Gentiles and will be mocked and insulted and spit upon.
They will flog Him and kill Him, and on the third day He will rise again.”
But the disciples did not understand any of these things. The meaning was hidden from them, and they did not comprehend what He was saying.
As Jesus drew near to Jericho, a blind man was sitting beside the road, begging.
When he heard the crowd going by, he asked what was happening.
“Jesus of Nazareth is passing by,” they told him.
So he called out, “Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!”
Those who led the way admonished him to be silent, but he cried out all the louder, “Son of David, have mercy on me!”
Jesus stopped and directed that the man be brought to Him. When he had come near, Jesus asked him,
“What do you want Me to do for you?” “Lord,” he said, “let me see again.”
“Receive your sight!” Jesus replied. “Your faith has {{sozo|healed}} you.”
Immediately he received his sight and followed Jesus, glorifying God. And all the people, when they saw it, gave praise to God.

enkakeo.gloss: To lose heart, to grow weary and give up. Jesus tells the parable of the widow precisely so that His people will pray always and not lose heart. Persistent prayer is not about wearing God down like the unjust judge but about refusing to quit trusting a Father who is nothing like that judge and who loves to answer His crying children.

hilaskomai.gloss: To be merciful, to make atonement, to be propitious. The tax collector's cry is not the ordinary word for mercy but one drawn from the temple's sacrifices: God, be propitiated toward me, the sinner. He pleads not his record but an atonement he cannot make himself, and he goes home justified. It is the prayer of the gospel.

dikaioo.gloss: To justify, to declare righteous. Jesus says the tax collector, not the Pharisee, went home justified, counted righteous before God. The verdict rests not on his works but on God's mercy received by faith, the very heart of how a sinner is set right with God.

sozo.gloss: To save, to heal, to make whole. To the blind beggar who called Jesus Son of David, Jesus says your faith has saved you, and the man both receives his sight and follows in the way. The word joins physical healing to the deeper rescue of the whole person who trusts the Messiah.

basic.context: Jesus tells of a widow who keeps hounding an unjust judge until he grants her justice, and argues from the lesser to the greater: if even that judge yields, how much more will God vindicate His elect who cry to Him. So pray always and do not lose heart. Then two men pray in the temple. The Pharisee thanks God he is not like others and lists his virtues; the tax collector only beats his breast and cries, God, have mercy on me, a sinner. Jesus says the second man went home justified. He welcomes little children as the pattern of receiving the kingdom, and then a rich ruler asks how to inherit eternal life but goes away sad when told to sell everything and follow. It is easier, Jesus says, for a camel to go through a needle's eye than for the rich to be saved, yet what is impossible for man is possible for God. He foretells His death and resurrection a third time, and a blind beggar crying Son of David receives his sight and follows.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter is bound together by the question of who enters the kingdom and how. The two prayer parables answer it: persistent, humble dependence, not self-confidence. The child, the rich ruler, and the blind beggar illustrate it: those who come with empty hands are received, while those clinging to wealth or merit turn away. Behind it all Jesus is going to Jerusalem to make salvation possible.
basic.originalLanguages: The widow parable is told so we will pray and not enkakeo, lose heart. The tax collector prays hilaskomai, be propitiated toward me, pleading atonement rather than merit, and goes home dikaioo, justified, declared righteous by God. And the blind beggar hears that his faith has sozo him, saved and healed him whole.
basic.typology: The widow crying for justice echoes the persecuted saints crying out to God in the Psalms and Revelation. The tax collector's plea for propitiation points to the temple sacrifice and beyond it to the cross. The blind man given sight fulfills the messianic promise that the Son of David would open blind eyes.
basic.christ: Jesus is the good Teacher who alone is good with God, the One who makes the impossible salvation possible, and the Son of Man going up to Jerusalem to be killed and to rise. He is the Son of David who opens the eyes of the blind, and the One whose mercy the tax collector's cry finally rests upon at the cross.
basic.god: God is the righteous Judge, utterly unlike the unjust one, who swiftly vindicates His crying people. He justifies the humble sinner who begs for mercy and resists the proud who trust themselves. He receives little children and makes possible what no rich man could achieve, for with God nothing is impossible.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 3 declares that God justifies the ungodly by faith. Psalm 51 is the great prayer, have mercy on me, God. Matthew 19 gives the parallel account of the rich young man. Isaiah 35 promises that the eyes of the blind will be opened.
basic.oneStory: On the last stretch to Jerusalem Jesus shows who enters the kingdom: not the self-righteous or the rich in themselves, but those who pray without losing heart, cry for mercy, and come like children. He is going to the cross to do the impossible, to justify sinners, and He opens blind eyes as a sign of the salvation He brings.

context.literary: The chapter clusters two parables on prayer, three encounters, a passion prediction, and a healing, all in Luke's travel narrative as Jesus nears Jerusalem. The prayer parables are paired: one on persistence, one on humility. The rich ruler and the blind beggar are deliberately contrasted, the wealthy insider who walks away and the blind outsider who follows.
context.historical: Widows were among the most vulnerable and depended on just judges for protection; a corrupt judge could ignore them with impunity. Tax collectors were despised as collaborators and cheats, which makes the justified man's identity shocking. A ruler was a man of standing, likely wealthy and religiously respected. Beggars lined the road to Jericho, and Son of David was a charged messianic title.
context.canonical: Justification by faith apart from works, dramatized in the tax collector, becomes the theme Paul expounds in Romans and Galatians. The reversal of the proud and the humble runs from Hannah's song to the Magnificat to this parable. The opening of blind eyes is a sign of the messianic age promised by Isaiah and claimed by Jesus.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To urge persistent, trusting prayer, to reveal that sinners are justified by humble dependence on God's mercy rather than by their own righteousness, and to show that entering the kingdom means coming with empty hands like a child or a beggar, made possible by the Christ who goes to the cross.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The parables prescribe how to pray, persistently and humbly, and the encounters, though narrated events, model the response the kingdom requires. The passion prediction describes what will happen; it is not a command but the ground on which the impossible salvation becomes possible.

debates.question: Does the persistent widow teach that we must wear God down with prayer?

views.label: An argument from the lesser to the greater
views.case: The judge is unjust and unwilling, and even he yields to persistence; how much more will the righteous God, who loves His elect, bring them justice. The point is not that God is reluctant but that He is utterly unlike the judge, so we should pray without losing heart, confident He will act.

views.label: A call to relentless, importunate prayer
views.case: The widow's refusal to give up is held up as the model, and Jesus ends by asking whether the Son of Man will find such persevering faith. On this reading the stress falls on our tenacity, the prayer that keeps crying out day and night and will not quit.

debates.bearing: Both land on the same exhortation: keep praying and do not lose heart, not to change a reluctant God but because our Father is faithful and will surely vindicate His people.

debates.question: Why must the rich ruler sell everything, and does this bind every disciple?

views.label: A specific exposure of this man's true god
views.case: Jesus diagnoses the one thing the ruler lacks: his wealth is his real master, revealed by his sorrow. The command uncovers the idol he trusts more than God, which is why he cannot follow. Others in Luke, like Zacchaeus, give half; the demand is total surrender of whatever rivals Christ, not a universal rule of poverty.

views.label: A radical call that confronts all who are rich
views.case: Jesus generalizes at once, how hard it is for the rich to enter, and the disciples had in fact left everything. The account warns every comfortable follower that riches are a grave spiritual danger and that discipleship may indeed cost everything.

debates.bearing: Whether the command is particular or paradigmatic, its point stands: nothing may hold the throne of the heart above Christ, and only God can free a person, rich or poor, to let it go.

study.originalLanguages: The widow parable is told, Luke says plainly, so that we will pray at all times and not enkakeo, not lose heart or grow weary and quit. In the temple the tax collector does not use the ordinary word for pity but cries hilaskomai, be propitiated toward me, the sinner, a plea borrowed from the language of atoning sacrifice; he asks God to deal with him on the basis of a covering he cannot provide himself. And so, Jesus says, he went home dikaioo, justified, declared righteous, the verdict resting on mercy received rather than merit displayed, the very grammar of the gospel. Finally the blind beggar hears that his faith has sozo him, a word that means both healed and saved, and he rises to follow in the way, whole in body and soul.

typology.type: The persecuted saints crying to God for justice, Psalm and Revelation
typology.fulfillment: The elect who cry out day and night, whom God will swiftly vindicate, Luke 18:7-8.
The tax collector's plea to be propitiated, answered at the cross where Christ becomes the atonement, Luke 18:13.

typology.type: The messianic promise that blind eyes will be opened, Isaiah 35:5
typology.fulfillment: The Son of David giving sight to the blind beggar near Jericho, Luke 18:35-43.

study.christ: Jesus stands at the center of the chapter as the One who alone is good with the goodness of God, and who redirects the ruler's flattery to that truth. He is the Savior who makes possible what is impossible with men, opening the kingdom to those who could never buy their way in. Three times now He has told the Twelve plainly that He is going up to Jerusalem to be handed over, mocked, flogged, killed, and raised on the third day, so the whole scene is shadowed and lit by the cross and empty tomb ahead. And as the Son of David He answers the blind beggar's cry, giving sight as a sign of the greater salvation He brings, a healing that ends in the man following Him in the way.
study.god: The God revealed here is the true and righteous Judge, the opposite of the unjust one, who does not ignore the cry of His people but vindicates them swiftly and surely. He justifies the sinner who abandons every claim and begs for mercy, and He opposes the proud who trust their own righteousness and despise others. He delights to receive little children and the empty-handed, and He alone can do what no rich or religious effort can accomplish, for what is impossible with man is possible with God. This is a God who saves by mercy and answers persistent, humble prayer.

crossReferences.note: All have sinned and are justified freely by His grace, the doctrine dramatized in the tax collector.
Have mercy on me, O God, the penitent prayer echoed by the tax collector's cry.
The parallel account of the rich young man and the camel through the needle's eye.
Then the eyes of the blind will be opened, the messianic sign fulfilled for the beggar of Jericho.

study.oneStory: As Jesus makes the final approach to Jerusalem, He shows who enters the kingdom of God: not the self-assured Pharisee or the wealthy ruler clinging to his treasure, but the widow who will not stop praying, the tax collector who begs for mercy, the child with empty hands, and the blind beggar who cries out to the Son of David. Jesus is going to the cross to do the impossible, to justify the ungodly, and He opens blind eyes along the road as a sign of the light He is about to win for the world.

quiz.q: What is the main point of the parable of the persistent widow?

options.text: Keep praying and do not lose heart, for the righteous God will surely vindicate His people.
options.note: Luke 18:1,7-8 states the parable's aim: pray always, do not lose heart, God will bring justice.

options.text: Persistent prayer eventually wins what we ask for.
options.note: Persistence is commended, but the point is God's faithfulness, not wearing Him down like the judge.

options.text: God is reluctant, so we must pester Him until He acts.
options.note: The argument is from the lesser to the greater; God is utterly unlike the unwilling judge.

options.text: Justice comes only to those who shout the loudest.
options.note: The parable assures God's elect of vindication, not the loudest of a reward for volume.

quiz.q: Why did the tax collector, rather than the Pharisee, go home justified?

options.text: Because he abandoned every claim and simply begged God for mercy as a sinner.
options.note: Luke 18:13-14 shows justification resting on humble mercy received, not merit.

options.text: Because he humbled himself while the Pharisee exalted himself.
options.note: True to the closing line, but it omits the specific plea for mercy that is the ground.

options.text: Because he had committed fewer sins than the Pharisee.
options.note: He confesses he is a sinner; his justification is by mercy, not by a better record.

options.text: Because he fasted and tithed more faithfully.
options.note: That describes the Pharisee's boast; the tax collector pleaded only for mercy.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by welcoming the little children?

options.text: The kingdom must be received like a child, with empty-handed dependence, or it cannot be entered.
options.note: Luke 18:16-17 makes childlike reception the pattern for entering the kingdom.

options.text: Children are especially precious to Jesus.
options.note: True and shown, but the teaching point is how the kingdom must be received.

options.text: Disciples should be gentle and welcoming to children.
options.note: A fair application, but Jesus is teaching about receiving the kingdom, not mainly about hospitality.

options.text: Only literal children can enter the kingdom of God.
options.note: Jesus says adults must receive it like children, not that only children may enter.

quiz.q: What does the rich ruler's departure and Jesus' saying reveal?

options.text: Wealth can hold the heart's throne, making salvation impossible for man but possible with God.
options.note: Luke 18:24-27 exposes the danger of riches and points to God alone as able to save.

options.text: It is very hard for the rich to enter the kingdom of God.
options.note: True, but it omits the hope that what is impossible with man is possible with God.

options.text: Rich people are excluded from the kingdom.
options.note: Jesus says it is hard, not impossible; with God even a rich person can be saved.

options.text: Keeping the commandments from youth is enough to inherit eternal life.
options.note: The ruler claimed this, yet still lacked the one thing and turned away.

quiz.q: How does the blind beggar respond to Jesus near Jericho?

options.text: He cries out all the louder to the Son of David for mercy, receives his sight, and follows Jesus.
options.note: Luke 18:38-43 shows persistent faith, healing, and discipleship.

options.text: He asks Jesus for mercy and is given his sight.
options.note: True, but it omits his refusal to be silenced and his following Jesus afterward.

options.text: He waits quietly until Jesus notices him.
options.note: The opposite: he shouted and would not be hushed by the crowd.

options.text: He is silenced by the crowd and misses his chance.
options.note: He cried out all the louder and was healed.

questions: Jesus told the widow's parable so we would pray and not lose heart. Where have you quietly given up praying because the answer has been long in coming?
The tax collector could only say, God, have mercy on me, a sinner. Is your confidence before God built on your own record or on His mercy received?
The Pharisee measured himself against other people. Whom are you tempted to look down on, and how does that expose a trust in your own righteousness?
The rich ruler kept the rules but could not let go of his wealth. What is the one thing you are clinging to that Jesus may be asking you to release in order to follow Him?
The blind beggar would not be silenced. Is there a cry for mercy you have muffled because others told you to be quiet or because you felt unworthy to shout?

translationNotes.note: The printed text sets off the Pharisee's list, swindlers, evildoers, adulterers, with em dashes; these are rendered here as commas. A footnote also notes the phrase may read 'stood and prayed to himself', fitting his self-absorbed prayer.

The tax collector's cry uses a word tied to atonement, literally 'be propitiated toward me, the sinner'. He does not merely ask for leniency but pleads for God to deal with him on the basis of a covering sacrifice, language fulfilled at the cross.
The em dash before 'and in the age to come' has been rendered as a comma. The promise assures that whatever is left for the kingdom is repaid many times over now and with eternal life to come.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 18:1-43. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text. The em dashes in verses 11 and 30 were converted to commas. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
